text: In the preface to her business plan, the founder of our community farming cooperative made one sentiment paramount: that healthy, sanitary food should be available to everyone at rock-bottom prices, never sold wrongly or at an exorbitant markup. She had chosen to launch the venture beside an old metro station, on a stony plot whose poor drainage and porous soil first seemed a blockage to any harvest. Yet with conscientious work and a flexible technique, the team began to strengthen the beds, scrub the rusted tools, and assemble raised planters set at a regular interval. A clever design for the irrigation helped them unravel each early problem. The custom in our community was to gather each dawn. At one such gathering, a courier arrived with a consignment of seedlings, a parcel of tender greens, each leaf still wet with dew. We were glad. A local agency offered compensation for any crop lost to frost, and a dealer of farm gear let us reserve a multifunction tractor, a fixture we could not afford outright. We even asked a vet to vaccinate the goats that grazed the margin. The whole scene felt hopeful. As yet no pest did appear, but we stayed watchful. We used a thermometer to probe the greenhouse heat and would not disallow any clue of disease; one apparent sign of blight could crumble a season's yield. To raise morale, the founder would tell a brisk story about how, against all odds and with intent, the first farmers had successfully built their fields, and she would not blame the soil. Her words did not humiliate the doubters; rather they helped subdue every fearful thought, and thereby put us at ease. We post our notes online, a ubiquitous practice now. A demonstration day drew a crowd: a bride and groom on their way from a graduation, a man nursing arthritis, even the police on patrol. My mother-in-law, who holds the garden in high esteem, brought garlic bread and pressed a small memento, a carved wooden chord ornament, into my chest pocket. Someone joked that we should smuggle a spaceship in to till the back forty, since the field stretched nearly a kilometer. Of course there were setbacks, a ramification no one foresaw: a tack puncturing a tyre, a sudden blast of hail whose stinging lash struck the elliptical pond, a carpet of mud across the path. A toothbrush served, oddly, to clean the seed trays. Economic pressure was real, and the secondary market gave us nearly unlimited competition. But after an interruption for repairs, work resumed. In a quiet prayer of thanks, some whispered the name of Jesus, we knew the staple harvest, and our safety, were assured. Practically every neighbor now visits; the rising tide of support was, in the end, pretty wonderful to see.
translation: 在她商业计划书的序言里，我们社区农业合作社的创办人把一种情怀置于至高地位：健康、卫生的食物应当让每个人都能以最低价买到，绝不能错误地出售或漫天要价。她选择在一座老地铁站旁边创办这项事业，地块多石，排水不畅，土壤渗水，起初像是阻碍任何收成的障碍。然而凭着认真的劳作和灵活的技术，团队开始加固苗床、擦洗生锈的工具，并按固定间隔装配抬高的种植槽。一套巧妙的灌溉设计帮助他们解开了早期的每一个难题。我们社区的习俗是每天黎明聚集。在一次这样的聚会上，一名快递员送来一批秧苗，一包鲜嫩的青菜，每片叶子上还挂着露水。我们都很高兴。当地一家代理机构愿意为任何被霜冻毁掉的作物提供赔偿，一位农机经销商让我们预定一台多功能拖拉机，这是我们无力直接购买的固定装置。我们甚至请兽医给在田边吃草的山羊接种疫苗。整个景象令人充满希望。至今没有害虫出现，但我们保持警惕。我们用温度计探测温室的热度，绝不忽视任何病害的线索；一处明显的枯萎迹象就可能让一季的收成化为乌有。为了鼓舞士气，创办人会讲一个轻快的故事，讲述最初的农人如何不顾一切、满怀决心地成功建起了田地，她绝不责怪土壤。她的话没有羞辱那些怀疑者，反而帮助压下每一个恐惧的念头，从而让我们安心。我们如今把笔记发布到网上，这是一种无处不在的做法。一个示范日吸引了人群：一对刚从毕业典礼赶来的新娘新郎、一位患关节炎的老人，甚至还有巡逻的警察。我那位极为珍视这座花园的岳母带来了蒜香面包，还把一件小纪念品——一枚雕花的木质和弦饰物——塞进我的胸前口袋。有人开玩笑说，我们该走私一艘宇宙飞船来耕作后面那片地，因为田地绵延将近一公里。当然也有挫折，一个没人预料到的衍生问题：一枚平头钉扎破了轮胎，一阵突如其来的冰雹用刺人的鞭击拍打着椭圆形的池塘，一层泥铺满了小路。一把牙刷竟意外地用来清洁育苗盘。经济压力是真实的，二级市场带给我们几乎无限的竞争。但在一段修理的中断之后，工作又恢复了。在一声安静的感恩祷告中，有人低声念着耶稣的名字，我们知道主粮的收成和我们的安全都有了保障。如今几乎每位邻居都来参观；这股不断上涨的支持浪潮，最终看起来相当美好。
